nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving and standing waters: blackwater streams, flooded forest pools, and shallow tributaries of the Rio Negro and Rio Branco drainages in the upper Amazon basin; typically over leaf litter and sandy-muddy substrate with overhanging vegetation

decorPreferences: driftwood and twisted roots for shelter
dried leaf litter (Indian almond, beech) for naturalistic blackwater biotope
overhanging plants (Amazon swords, Echinodorus) and floating cover
caves or PVC pipes as refuge

diet.notes: Accepts a wide range of prepared and live/frozen foods. Offer sinking wafers (algae wafers, catfish pellets), frozen or live bloodworms, tubifex, brine shrimp, daphnia, and blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber, spinach). Graze on biofilm and detritus naturally. Feed primarily at dusk or after lights-out due to crepuscular/nocturnal habits. Varied diet improves condition and coloration.

goodWith: other peaceful South American tetras (cardinal tetras, rummy-nose tetras)
dwarf cichlids (Apistogramma spp.) in suitably-sized tanks
avoidWith: very small nano fish or invertebrates that may be consumed
boisterous or fast-moving species that outcompete for food

summary: The Flagtail Catfish is a larger Amazonian callichthyid (~15 cm) prized for its striking black-and-white vertically banded caudal fin. A sociable, peaceful bottom-dweller that breathes atmospheric air via intestinal respiration; best kept in groups in well-decorated blackwater biotopes.
careNotes:
Dianema urostriatum is one of two species in the genus Dianema and is the larger representative of the Callichthyidae family — reaching up to 15 cm, roughly three times the length of a typical Corydoras. The common name ‘Flagtail’ refers to its unmistakable caudal fin, which bears bold black vertical stripes on a pale background, resembling a waving flag and easily distinguishing it from the related Porthole Catfish (Dianema longibarbis), which has rounded spots rather than stripes.

As a callichthyid, the Flagtail Catfish is an intestinal air-breather and must have unobstructed access to the water surface; ensure the tank is covered but allows the fish to gulp air freely. Sudden changes in barometric pressure or deteriorating water quality trigger more frequent surfacing — a useful early-warning indicator. Like all callichthyids it possesses sharp, lockable pectoral and dorsal fin spines that are mildly venomous and can cause a painful wound; handle with care using a soft mesh net.

A group of at least four specimens is recommended: lone individuals are reclusive and stress-prone, while groups produce active, visible shoaling behaviour in the lower to mid zones. A tank of 150 L or more with a footprint of at least 100 cm length is suitable for a group of four to six. Soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 2–15) replicates the blackwater conditions of their Rio Negro and Rio Branco native drainages; temperature 22–28°C. The addition of Indian almond leaves or peat filtration is beneficial but not strictly required. Provide a soft sandy substrate, ample hiding places (driftwood, leaf litter, caves), and subdued lighting or floating plants to reduce stress. Regular partial water changes of 25–30% weekly are recommended to maintain water quality.
breedingNotes: Breeding in the aquarium has been documented but is uncommon and generally considered moderately difficult. Males develop a broader, thicker pectoral spine and more intensely striped tail when in breeding condition. Spawning typically occurs in bubble nests built under broad leaves or floating debris — behaviour unusual among callichthyids. The male constructs and guards the nest. Eggs hatch in approximately 4–5 days at 26°C. Fry are small and should be fed infusoria and finely crushed prepared foods initially, progressing to micro-worms and newly hatched brine shrimp nauplii. Separating fry from adults improves survival rates. Conditioning breeders with live and frozen foods prior to attempted spawning is advisable.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Dianema urostriatum
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (~14 cm), temperature 22–28°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 18–215 ppm (approx. gH 1–12), native range (Rio Negro and Rio Branco drainages, Brazil), habitat (slow/standing blackwater), diet (omnivore, sinking foods), social behaviour (group-kept), and breeding notes (bubble nest builder, male guards).

additional.name: FishBase — Dianema urostriatum
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 15 cm TL), taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), native range (upper Amazon basin, Brazil: Rio Negro and tributaries), temperature range 22–28°C, and diet (omnivore). Confirmed venomous pectoral spines (as is standard for Callichthyidae). Lifespan estimated 5–10 years based on comparable callichthyids.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Catfish Care Guides
additional.notes: General callichthyid and catfish husbandry guidance consulted for tank setup, feeding schedule, and community recommendations applicable to Dianema. No dedicated Flagtail Catfish page available at Aquarium Co-Op at time of access.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Dianema species profile
additional.notes: Supplementary source for species identification notes distinguishing D. urostriatum (vertical caudal stripes) from D. longibarbis (round spots), air-breathing behaviour notes, handling cautions regarding spine venom, and community compatibility guidance.

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Dianema urostriatum care
additional.notes: Supplementary hobbyist-facing source consulted for tank size recommendations, group-keeping behaviour, and blackwater biotope setup guidance. Minimum group size of 4 and 150 L tank size corroborated.

fish.breedingNotes: Bubble-nest builder; male constructs and guards nest under floating debris or broad leaves. Eggs hatch in ~4–5 days at 26°C. Fry require infusoria then micro-worms/baby brine shrimp. Separate fry from adults to improve survival. Condition breeders with live/frozen foods. Uncommon in the hobby but achievable in a species or single-pair setup.
